Article Title: 86% of high-net-worth Indians now use AI for investing, highest in the world: HSBC research
Article Text:
India tops global AI adoption in finance

Why Indian investors trust AI for research but not the final call

The hybrid model shaping the future of wealth management

Indian investors are adopting AI for money matters faster than anyone else in the world. Yet when the moment comes to actually commit, they still want a human in the room.That's the takeaway from new HSBC research, conducted by Ipsos, surveying nearly 10,000 affluent and high-net-worth individuals across 10 markets. India's slice—1,115 respondents—stood out. A striking 86% said they use AI specifically for finance and investing, the highest of any market surveyed and well above the 73% global average.The appeal is mostly about legwork. Indians lean on AI for analysis and research (80%), strategy support (70%), and to sense-check their own thinking (32%). The payoff shows up in confidence: 36% feel steadier walking into a conversation with their financial adviser.But adoption isn't the same as authority. Financial professionals remain the top source of investment ideas (67%) and the single biggest influence on final decisions (31%)—more than double AI's 15%. Investors value advisers for things a model can't fake, namely emotional reassurance (89%) and strategic expertise (78%).Most see the two working side by side. Over half (51%) prefer a hybrid setup: use AI to explore, then bring in a human to validate before acting.The confidence boost has a behavioural edge too. Indian investors are the boldest globally, with 64% saying AI makes them more willing to take calculated risks, against a 49% global average."AI isn't replacing professional advice—it's raising the quality of decision-making," said Sandeep Batra, Head of International Wealth and Premier Banking at HSBC India. He framed the future as a partnership where AI speeds up analysis and advisers bring judgement and accountability when it counts.
Article Summary: Indian investors are adopting AI for money matters faster than anyone else in the world. But adoption isn't the same as authority. Investors value advisers for things a model can't fake, namely emotional reassurance and strategic expertise. Over half prefer a hybrid setup: use AI to explore, then bring in a human.

Article Title: Top Google DeepMind engineer to students entering college and wanting to join OpenAI, Anthropic and other AI labs: Work like a dog, take ...
Article Text:
Top Google DeepMind engineer to students entering college and wanting to join OpenAI, Anthropic and other AI labs: Work like a dog, take ...

"How to Land a Frontier Lab Job"

“Work like a dog. Take difficult, proof-based classes. Code, obviously. Use AI for what you already know how to do, only, but aggressively so,"

Google DeepMind senior engineer’s advice for college students looking to work at OpenAI, Anthropic and other AI labs

“Give up your weekends and nights. Burned into my mind is the typical workflow my college friends and I have gone through.

We would start from the very morning of Saturday with two big quadruple-shot Panera iced coffees until late, then come back and do it again the following day, hoping to finish early enough to trudge back to our rooms on the other side of campus to get to sleep on time for the start of another week of psets.

Rolling deadlines collapse on each other with nothing but another such weekend to look forward to. Outside, clear blue skies host a warm sun shining over the Cottage Club, an eating club we never set foot in, where, locked in our tower atop Lewis Library, the blasting music from its backyard reverberated off the windows as Sunday Funday proceeded differently for those inside versus outside.

Not for you. Get that soft sponge in your head, which was mostly a primitive state machine tuned by evolution to hunt and eat and fuck, to think abstractly. Learn to build thought.”

Why Google DeepMind senior engineer thinks it's difficult to get into top AI labs

"There is always a vanguard of elite college students (both undergrads and PhDs) who do ML research in top-tier conferences, math and programming competitions, and already have connections to these labs through older classmates or friends,"

Google DeepMind's senior engineer Vladimir Feinberg has advice for college students looking to work at frontier artificial intelligence (AI) labs such as OpenAI , Anthropic, and Google DeepMind. Sharing his guidance in a blog post titled, he wrote that success in the field requires intense dedication, rigorous academic training and sustained effort.Explaining why it's so tough to succeed in this field, Feinberg, an engineer who leads Gemini pre-training at Google DeepMind, noted that competition for positions at leading AI research labs has intensified as some of the most academically accomplished students pursue careers in artificial intelligence.Feinberg wrote while outlining what he would tell himself if he were entering college today.In a recent episode of The Peterman Pod, Feinberg said that after publishing his blog post, he heard from employees at both OpenAI and Anthropic who agreed with the advice, suggesting that the recommendations resonate across multiple frontier AI labs.For students entering college and hoping to eventually work at leading AI labs, Feinberg advised fully committing to academic and technical development.Giving reference from his own experience, Feinberg wrote,According to Feinberg, securing a role at a frontier AI lab is difficult because candidates compete against highly accomplished undergraduate and PhD students who are already publishing machine learning research, participating in mathematics and programming competitions, and building professional networks at major AI companies.He noted that many of the students now targeting companies such as OpenAI, Anthropic and Google DeepMind previously might have pursued careers at quantitative finance firms such as Citadel and Jane Street.Feinberg said there is a group of students who gain an early advantage by understanding which problems matter in AI research and spending years sharpening their technical skills.he wrote.According to Feinberg, these candidates often stand out for three qualities: intent, mathematical maturity, and grit. He argued that the ability to solve complex problems, focus on important research areas, and handle demanding workloads is a strong indicator of future success.
Article Summary: Google DeepMind senior engineer Vladimir Feinberg has advice for college students looking to work at frontier artificial intelligence (AI) labs such as OpenAI and Anthropic. He wrote that success in the field requires intense dedication, rigorous academic training and sustained effort. Feinberg noted that competition for positions at leading AI research labs has intensified.

Article Title: Google engineer reportedly sacked over viral Workspace CLI AI tool, says: 'The fear wasn't my tool, it was agents as...'
Article Text:
Ex-Googler says Google fired him over his viral Workspace CLI—two days after Google announced its own.

What actually got him fired, in his words

Why command-line tools became the new battleground for AI agents

Ex-Googler Justin Poehnelt built the thing his company would announce it wanted two days later. His Google Workspace CLI climbed to No. 1 on Hacker News, drew thousands of GitHub stars and racked up thousands of users inside a couple of days. Then Google fired him. The engineer, who'd spent nearly seven years there, told the story himself this week in an X thread that has since blown past 2.6 million views, and the timing is the part doing the heavy lifting. Two days before they fired him, Google Cloud Next announced that an official Workspace CLI was coming.He says he wanted it out in his own words, partly to explain it and partly to own it, and partly because saying it aloud is part of how he heals.The tool, called gws, was clever in a way developers immediately got. Rather than ship a fixed menu of commands, it reads Google's own Discovery Service at runtime and dynamically builds its entire command surface from there. One interface, both human- and agent-friendly, sitting on top of Gmail, Drive, Calendar, Docs and every other Workspace API.Poehnelt doesn't think the CLI itself was the real trigger. His reading is blunter than that."I think the cause was that Workspace and certain leaders (and projects) were afraid of being disrupted. But the fear wasn't specific to my CLI, it was a broader fear in what agents meant for Workspace."The branding question is where it gets genuinely murky. Legal grilled him about why Google's logo and brand colors appeared on a Google Workspace GitHub repository—a question whose irony is somewhat hard to miss given that the project lived under the official googleworkspace GitHub organization. Critics on Hacker News have a fair point that releasing a project with Google's logo without going through brand approval was a policy violation regardless of how technically impressive it was. Poehnelt hasn't disputed that the process wasn't followed. What he disputes is that the branding was the reason rather than the cover for one.He also batted away the assumption that this was a hobby. Asked on X whether it was a 20% side project, he said no—he was on Workspace Developer Relations, the team whose regular job is building open-source layers and abstractions over Google's APIs. This was the work.For all that, he carries strikingly little bitterness. Nearly seven years at Google was a real opportunity, he said, crediting good teammates and a manager who backed him to the end.The irony has a structural cause. The command line has become prime real estate in the agent race, because tools like Claude Code proved agents work best through an interface that's scriptable, inspectable and already installed everywhere. An agent that can drive Workspace from a terminal is also an agent that can route around Google's own sanctioned surfaces—a real edge for developers, a real headache for whoever owns the product roadmap.Google knows it. At Cloud Next, CEO Thomas Kurian declared that "the era of the pilot is over. The era of the agent is here." The uncomfortable footnote is that the engineer who'd already shipped exactly that was on his way out. He wasn't the only one to leave, either—Addy Osmani, the director whose post Poehnelt calls "the tweet that got me fired," has since departed Google as well.
Article Summary: Ex-Googler Justin Poehnelt built the thing his company would announce it wanted two days later. His Google Workspace CLI climbed to No. 1 on Hacker News, drew thousands of GitHub stars. The engineer, who'd spent nearly seven years there, told the story himself.

Article Title: In 1957, the Wham-O founders, watching a pie tin sail across a lawn, unexpectedly helped create the Frisbee we still use today
Article Text:
A Frisbee made by Wham-O. Image Credits: Wikimedia Commons

A campus tradition before the Frisbee

From a pie tin to a plastic disc

Walter Frederick Morrison promoting his Pluto Platters. Image Credits: Wikimedia Commons

Why 1957 became a turning point

Why the Frisbee matters

Innovation does not always begin with a breakthrough. Sometimes it begins with people having fun. In the case of the Frisbee, a pastime that started with students tossing pie tins, eventually caught the attention of entrepreneurs who saw more than a flying object. They saw a product. By 1957, that vision had helped turn a simple lawn game into a toy that would become a global phenomenon.The Frisbee's story is unusual because no single person can claim to have invented it outright. Its origins stretch from university campuses, where students discovered the surprising flight of discarded pie tins, to inventors who experimented with plastic discs, and finally to the Wham-O company, which refined and popularised the idea.The roots of the Frisbee stretch back decades before Wham-O entered the picture. According to Yale University's historical records, students were tossing empty pie tins from the Frisbie Pie Company as early as the 1920s. As the tins sailed through the air, students reportedly shouted "Frisbee!" to warn others to look out.This became a routine on the university campus. Although the pie plates were not invented as toys, their shape was ideal for playing with them in this manner.Slowly, the process of throwing the flying discs became an integral part of student culture, laying the foundation for a commercially successful product.What is important about this particular history of the Frisbee is that it proves the Frisbee was not a result of an accidental invention. The basic idea had already been explored, but the challenge was to create something lighter and safer.The next significant contribution was made by inventor Fred Morrison, who started working on plastic flying discs in the 1940s. Unlike metal pie plates, these devices were stronger, more easily thrown, and better at flying.According to Illumin Magazine the chain runs from the Frisbiepie company to Yale students, then to Fred Morrison’s plastic flying disc. Morrison eventually invented the "Pluto Platter," which was a plastic flying disc that was partially designed based on the craze about flying saucers that existed among Americans at that time. In 1955, Morrison sold the invention rights to Wham-O, a California-based toy company that was known for developing unusual ideas into toys.For Wham-O, it meant the development of an already existing idea and not the creation of something new. Wham-O realised that people loved throwing discs, but the difficulty lay in developing a toy out of it.In 1957, Wham-O was manufacturing and distributing flying discs on a broader scale than ever before. They named their new product "Frisbee" partly because this was intended to differentiate the product from the Frisbie Pie Company.What made this moment significant was not the discovery that discs could fly. People had known that for decades. Instead, the secret lay in recognising that a simple recreational activity could become a successful consumer product. Wham-O standardised the design, created a memorable brand, and introduced the flying disc to a much wider audience.The company effectively transformed a traditional game into a commercial toy. A pastime that had existed on university lawns was suddenly available in toy shops across America.The Frisbee endures because of its simple design: it needs no batteries, screens, or complicated instructions. Its history can be considered an example of how innovation occurs. The Frisbee was created by a long series of developments; it was thrown around campus lawns by students using pie tins, and after several inventors tried out different materials, entrepreneurs managed to identify the market value of an existing product.In the end, what we have today is an item which has changed little since then. More than sixty years after Wham-O popularised the flying disc, the Frisbee still has the same charm it had when people watched a spinning disc sail across a lawn.
Article Summary: The Frisbee's story is unusual because no single person can claim to have invented it outright. Its origins stretch from university campuses, to inventors who experimented with plastic discs, and finally to the Wham-O company, which refined and popularised the idea. The company effectively transformed a traditional game into a commercial toy.

Article Title: Japan’s second richest man SoftBank’s Masayoshi Son junks his plan to retire in sixties; says: 'I have become greedier and would …'
Article Text:
Son dismisses ‘ AI bubble ’ fears as blasphemy

Poll What impact do you think AI will have on jobs in the next decade? Job creation will outweigh job loss. More jobs will be at risk.

SoftBank Group founder and CEO Masayoshi Son has said that he is not planning to step down in his sixties, announcing to shareholders that he intends to remain at the helm of the technology conglomerate for another decade or more. According to a report by news agency Reuters, the 68-year-old billionaire tech investor declared that he no longer has time for retirement as he shifts SoftBank’s focus toward artificial intelligence (AI) and transforming the company into an AI-powered robotics powerhouse.“I have become greedier. I would like to do more over the next 10 to 15 years. I will stay healthy as long as I can,” Son told an audience of retail investors and shareholders.At a time when Wall Street and global investors are debating whether the massive tech rally is sustainable, Son defended the sector’s current valuations. Having lived through both the dot-com crash and massive portfolio swings during the COVID-19 pandemic, the veteran dismissed any talk of a market downturn.“I think it’s blasphemy against AI if you say it’s a bubble. It’s just the beginning. AI’s potential will be unlocked,” Son stated during the annual general meeting, as per Reuters.Son revealed that he has revised his 50-year business roadmap to lead SoftBank well into his 70s. His ultimate goal is to help bring about “artificial superintelligence,” a level of AI that he defines as being 10,000 times smarter than a human being.SoftBank already owns an approximately 90% stake in British chip designer Arm Holdings, and has committed an estimated $65 billion to investing into ChatGPT-maker OpenAI . Beyond pure software, SoftBank is pushing into physical AI and automation through several key initiatives, including an agreement to acquire ABB’s robotics unit at a valuation of roughly $5.4 billion.The conglomerate is also reported to be in early discussions to participate in an $800 million funding round for German startup Agile Robots, and there have been reports that SoftBank plans to establish and list a brand-new AI and robotics company called Roze in the US as early as this year.
Article Summary: Masayoshi Son, founder and CEO of SoftBank Group, has said that he is not planning to step down in his sixties. The 68-year-old billionaire tech investor declared that he no longer has time for retirement as he shifts SoftBank’s focus toward artificial intelligence (AI)

Article Title: In 1991, Quentin Stafford-Fraser and Paul Jardetzky set up a camera to watch a coffee pot, laying the groundwork for the world’s first webcam
Article Text:
Quentin Stafford-Fraser, one of the Cambridge researchers who helped create the coffee-pot monitoring system that evolved into the world's first webcam. Image Credits: Wikipedia

A coffee pot, a camera, and a simple problem

From a fun experiment to the first

webcam

The XCoffee software is displaying a live image of the Trojan Room coffee pot. Image Credits: Wikipedia

An ordinary innovation with an extraordinary legacy

Long before video calls, livestreams, and security cameras became part of everyday life, a group of researchers at the University of Cambridge faced a far simpler problem: finding out whether there was any coffee left in the communal pot. Their solution was practical, almost humorous, and completely unplanned.A project designed to save researchers from unnecessary walks soon evolved into the first webcam, demonstrating the potential of real-time image sharing over networks.The story began in the Trojan Room, a shared workspace in Cambridge University's Computer Laboratory. Researchers frequently walked to the communal coffee machine only to discover that the pot was empty.In a 2001 retrospective published in Communications of the ACM, Fraser recalled that the project emerged from the researchers' frustration with repeatedly finding an empty coffee pot. Rather than making unnecessary trips, the team devised a way to check the pot's status by using an unused camera, a frame-grabber card, and custom software.Fraser developed the software used to capture images of the coffee pot and display them on the computers of the laboratory's network.Jardetzky collaborated in order to build the system, which helped set up the camera and made the process of remote checking of the coffee pot possible. They jointly created a system where images of the coffee pot were captured and displayed on the computers within the laboratory. The researchers could check whether any coffee was left without leaving their offices.Despite its simplicity, the technology introduced an innovative concept: a camera could supply a stream of constantly updated images of a real object to users in another location. Instead of a photo, the users could monitor the changes as they occurred.The coffee-pot camera continued to be used locally by researchers in Cambridge for about two years. As mentioned in a timeline maintained by the University of Cambridge , the system took a significant step forward in November 1993 when researcher Daniel Gordon adapted the software to respond to web requests. This change allowed users outside the laboratory to view the coffee-pot feed on the World Wide Web, turning the project into what is widely regarded as the world’s first webcam.Visitors from all corners of the globe could log on to find out about the status of a coffee pot in the laboratory at Cambridge University. The topic may have been trivial, but the technology was revolutionary. For the first time in history, users were able to access live picture feeds from any location using a web browser. The webcam quickly became a curiosity of the early internet and attracted viewers far beyond the university.The origin of the webcam is a reminder that big innovations do not always come from ambitiously designed plans but as a result of solving mundane problems creatively.Stafford-Fraser and Jardetzky did not intend to create a device which would revolutionise the communications industry. Their only intention was to check whether any coffee was left without leaving the laboratory. Nevertheless, their solution helped demonstrate a technology that later became central to digital life.More than three decades later, billions of people use webcams for work, education, entertainment, and social interaction. The path to those technologies can be traced back to a small room in Cambridge, where a camera pointed at a coffee pot unexpectedly opened a window into a more connected world.
Article Summary: Researchers at the University of Cambridge used a coffee pot to monitor whether any coffee had been left in the lab. They used an unused camera, a frame-grabber card, and custom software to capture images of the coffee pot and display them on the laboratory's computers. The webcam quickly became a curiosity of the early internet and attracted viewers far beyond Cambridge.

Article Title: In 1971, Federico Faggin and Ted Hoff fitted a calculator brain onto one chip, invented the microprocessor that led to the personal computers we still use today
Article Text:
The Intel 4004 microprocessor, mounted on a Busicom calculator circuit board, alongside supporting chips that formed one of the earliest commercial microprocessor systems. Image Credits: Wikimedia

A calculator project with unexpected consequences

The engineers behind the innovation

Federico Faggin, the engineer who led the design of the Intel 4004. Image Credits: Wikipedia

The birth of the microprocessor era

Why the invention still matters

Long before laptops, smartphones, and personal computers became part of everyday life, computers were large, expensive machines built from numerous separate components. In 1969, a small team at Intel working on a calculator project took a different approach. By placing the central processing functions of a computer onto a single silicon chip, they created the Intel 4004, widely recognised as the world's first commercial microprocessor.A solution designed for a single calculator soon became a foundation of modern computing.The story began in 1969 when the Japanese company Busicom approached Intel with a request for custom chips for a new electronic calculator. Instead of designing numerous specialised chips, Intel proposed a more flexible solution. The company would create a small set of four chips, including one that could be programmed to perform different tasks.This idea was a major departure from the fixed-function electronic circuits commonly used at the time. Rather than being built for a single purpose, the new chip could execute instructions and perform different operations. As described by Federico Faggin in Nature Electronics , that programmable chip eventually became the Intel 4004, the world's first commercial microprocessor.Federico Faggin was one of the key figures behind the design and development process, and his colleague Ted Hoff contributed to the conception of the architecture that made such an innovation possible. Working alongside fellow engineer Stanley Mazor, the team sought to integrate the essential functions of a computer's central processing unit onto a single silicon chip.In addition to managing the project, Federico Faggin developed the silicon-gate technology and design methods that made reliable processors possible. As a result, Intel created the 4004 chip, a 4-bit processor with about 2,300 transistors. Although processors now have billions of transistors and can perform far more complex tasks, the novelty in 1971 was showing that an entire central processing unit could fit on one chip.Though the Intel 4004 was originally meant for calculators rather than personal computers, it marked the start of a new generation of technology. By proving that all of a computer’s operations could fit into one chip, the 4004 helped launch the microprocessor era.After the success of the 4004, Intel produced even more efficient microprocessors such as the 8008 and the 8080. These microprocessors had additional capabilities and served as building blocks for the creation of personal computers and other electronic devices.The study on Microprocessors: the engines of the digital age , traces a direct technological lineage from the 4004 to these later processors and the broader microprocessor ecosystem that followed. This progression was crucial because personal computers required processors that were smaller, cheaper, and more flexible before they could become widely accessible.Nevertheless, historians of technology point out that the development of the Intel 4004 was not a direct result of the creation of a personal computer. Instead, it helped inspire a new approach to building computers. By proving that a processor could be sold as a separate commercial product, the 4004 laid the groundwork for later generations of computing.Today, many devices we use daily rely on principles first demonstrated in the Intel 4004. From phones to laptops, the technology we use in the modern world is based on the invention of fifty years ago. In short, the Intel 4004 became the basis for many digital technologies used today
Article Summary: The Intel 4004 was the world's first commercial microprocessor. It was designed for a calculator and proved that a central processing unit could fit on one chip. The 4004 laid the groundwork for later generations of computing. From phones to laptops, the technology we use today is based on the invention of fifty years ago.

Article Title: In 1974, George Laurer refined black bars on a grocery label and developed the barcode system behind modern retail scanning
Article Text:
George Laurer, the IBM engineer who refined the Universal Product Code (UPC) barcode that became the global standard for retail product identification. Image Credits: Wikipedia

From a grocery problem to a

retail revolution

The scan that changed shopping

A UPC barcode is the simple pattern of black bars that became one of the most widely used technologies in modern commerce. Image Credits: Wikipedia

Why the barcode still matters

Every day, billions of products move through supermarkets, warehouses, and delivery networks with the help of a simple pattern of black bars. Most shoppers barely notice them. Still, these small markings transformed retail by allowing products to be identified and tracked in seconds.The barcode was not the result of a single flash of inspiration.Instead, it was the result of decades of experimentation, refinement, and standardisation. At the centre of that story was George Laurer, an engineer whose work helped turn an ambitious idea into a practical system that changed the way goods are bought and sold around the world.The origins of the barcode trace back to an ordinary problem. In the late 1940s, supermarkets were struggling with slow checkout lines and labor-intensive inventory management. Retailers wanted a way to identify products quickly and accurately without relying on manual entry.Inventors Norman Woodland and Bernard Silver proposed one of the earliest machine-readable coding systems and received a patent in 1952. However, the technology of the time was not yet capable of supporting widespread adoption.More than two decades later, George Laurer helped solve that problem. Working at IBM, he refined and standardised the Universal Product Code (UPC), creating a format that could be printed easily, scanned reliably, and used across thousands of stores and products.The breakthrough was not merely the barcode itself, but the creation of a common standard that retailers, manufacturers, and technology companies could all adopt.According to Smithsonian magazine , the barcode's defining moment came on June 26, 1974, when the first Universal Product Code (UPC) was successfully scanned at a supermarket in Troy, Ohio. It was no longer an experimental piece of technology but a real-life solution that demonstrated the possibility for machines to read product data in a shopping cart.The product involved was remarkably ordinary: a 10-pack of Wrigley’s Juicy Fruit chewing gum. Today, that packet is preserved at the National Museum of American History, not because of its contents, but because it marked the beginning of a retail revolution. For the first time, a standardised code allowed a machine to instantly identify a product and retrieve its information.That is what persuaded retailers to adopt the system, from faster checkout lines and fewer pricing errors to better inventory management. Something as simple as a series of black stripes and numbers has changed the world of trading and retailing beyond recognition within a couple of decades.Many decades later, however, the UPC remains one of the most successful standards ever created for retail use. Although various barcodes have been developed since the 1970s, they are all based on the same principle established at that time.Barcodes are convenient due to their simplicity. They are cheap to print, easy to scan and reliable enough to work with any type of goods, from products bought in groceries to shipping cartons. With time, barcodes became much more than just a method of checking out goods from a shop. They have become indispensable tools in managing inventories, tracking shipments, analysing sales trends and organising supply chains.As the study, Utility of QR codes in biological collections notes, George Laurer did more than refine a pattern of black bars. He helped create a practical system that could work in supermarkets, warehouses, and supply chains around the world. Its success came from its utility: a fast and dependable way to identify products and manage information.Today, the black bars on everyday products are so common that they are often taken for granted. Still, the history of barcodes shows how breakthroughs can emerge from ordinary problems.The need to speed up grocery checkout helped drive the creation of this universal code system.
Article Summary: The first Universal Product Code (UPC) was scanned at a supermarket in Troy, Ohio, on June 26, 1974. The product involved was a 10-pack of Wrigley’s Juicy Fruit chewing gum. For the first time, a standardised code allowed a machine to instantly identify a product and retrieve its information.

Article Title: Scientists just found that more than one-third of 500,000 ChatGPT conversations were used to generate fiction, and the real surprise is how often power users kept asking for the same stories in new va
Article Text:
A digital revolution is underway as AI transforms reading into interactive world-building. Thousands are now co-creating stories, fanfiction, and erotica with chatbots, moving beyond passive consumption. Image Credits: ChatGPT

Fascinating rise of the solitary storyteller

Researchers observed 'infinite story demanders' who endlessly refine narratives. This shift offers immediate gratification and democratizes creativity, though some fear it may diminish collective cultural experiences. Image Credits: ChatGPT

How might this impact human imagination in the future?

For decades, such enchantment was only possible in one direction, where the universe is brought to life by a faraway writer, and all a reader needed was to take everything that had been offered. But there has been a kind of revolution happening in private rooms and offices around the globe without much noise, thanks to digital technology. Rather than visiting bookstores, thousands of people decided to be co-creators thanks to AI.This dramatic shift from traditional reading to interactive world-building is no longer just an assumption or a passing trend. A research paper titled AI Fiction in the Wild has pulled back the curtain on this hidden digital subculture, examining a massive dataset of over five hundred thousand anonymised, English-language ChatGPT conversations. The study discovered that more than one-third of these interactions were dedicated entirely to producing creative stories, roleplay scenarios, fanfiction, and erotica. The findings suggest that chatbots are being used less as research tools and more as collaborative writing partners.What surprised the researchers most was not the amount of creative writing, but the behaviour of a particular group of power users.By coining a term for these users, the authors called them "infinite story demanders. While typical users asked for one complete story and moved on, these users returned to the same threads for weeks or months, asking for new twists or endings.When you read a book or watch a television show, you have little influence over how your favourite characters are treated, and if something bothers you, there is not much you can do about it. People appear to be using artificial intelligence to solve that problem.The data highlights a bias toward established worlds, with fanfiction accounting for forty-nine per cent of the fiction requests and erotica making up twenty-seven per cent. Users routinely input their favourite pop culture figures, anime characters, or completely original personas into the prompt box, treating the interface as a highly responsive sandbox. The study describes this pattern as a form of 'solipsistic reader-writer' behaviour. This describes a new kind of consumer who generates and enjoys fiction in a largely private loop.The cycle produces immediate gratification that cannot be matched by conventional forms of publishing. The user can call for a story that is extremely specific and get it on the spot. The user can then call for a different take on the same characters and get it instantly.These behaviour patterns pose interesting questions about how our culture might change in the coming decade. Certain cultural critics fear that machine-made fictional worlds might lessen the collective experience of reading. Certain critics fear that individualised entertainment could hinder our ability to consume difficult forms of art.Others see the phenomenon as a democratisation of creativity. People who felt alienated by media or lacked writing skills have found a way to create art and connect emotionally through this technology. They can enjoy creating worlds with few practical limits.In summary, the time and energy put into the creation of virtual fantasy worlds serve as a reminder of something that is inherently human. No matter the means used, man continues to tell stories. This is because storytelling is an essential aspect of human culture. Simply put, man uses modern technology to enhance his ability to tell stories.
Article Summary: Researchers observed 'infinite story demanders' who endlessly refine narratives. This shift offers immediate gratification and democratizes creativity. Some critics fear that machine-made fictional worlds might lessen the collective experience of reading. Others see the phenomenon as a democratisation of creativity.

Article Title: America's 89-year-old company Polaroid is 'warning' Americans about datacenters; says: Go jump in some water before…
Article Text:
Image credit: X (Twitter)

"Go jump in some water before the data centers drink it all up.”

"the best of summer is analog,"

"There'll come a day when the things we took for granted can never be taken again.

Obviously, we're being a bit cheeky with our line here, but still. Go take a swim. Go take a stroll. Go embrace the beautiful, simple, wild, analog stuff. That's where the best of summer, and life, lives.”

Polaroid says it is not anti-digital



“While our campaigns are provocative and challenge our relationship with technology, we're not anti-digital. We know we have to live alongside it, but we're deeply pro-human, and know what humanity gives us. And we know what we stand to lose if we don't protect it. That's a fight worth fighting.”

Why AI data centres are under scrutiny



“Water is totally fake. It used to be true, we used to do evaporative cooling in data centers, but now that we don't do that, you know, you see these like things on the internet where, 'Don't use ChatGPT, it's 17 gallons of water for each query' or whatever,”

Artificial intelligence (AI) datacentres have faced significant criticism in the US. The latest warning for them came from 89-year-old camera company Polaroid, which has launched a new advertising campaign highlighting concerns over the water consumption of data centres that power AI technologies.The company recently unveiled a billboard at Coney Island Beach in Brooklyn, New York, carrying the message:The billboard is part of Polaroid's global marketing campaign,which coincides with the launch of its new Go Generation 3 camera.In a post shared on Instagram , the company, elaborating on the campaign's message, wrote,Polaroid's latest campaign marks another effort by the company to encourage consumers to spend more time engaging with offline activities and less time connected to digital platforms. While the campaign challenges people's relationship with technology, Polaroid said it is not opposed to digital products and services.In a statement to Business Insider, Polaroid's creative director, Patricia Varella, said,The campaign adds to the broader discussion around the environmental impact of AI infrastructure, particularly the resources required to operate large-scale data centres.Data centres consume water both directly for cooling servers and indirectly through electricity generation. Concerns about their environmental footprint have increased as AI adoption continues to grow.A previous Business Insider investigation found that some of the largest data centre facilities in the US were permitted to use more water per day than nearly 49,000 Americans typically consume.Technology companies, however, argue that newer cooling technologies are helping reduce water usage. Earlier this year, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman dismissed some of the claims surrounding AI's water consumption.Altman said, when asked about concerns over AI companies' water use.Meanwhile, Nvidia recently introduced a liquid-cooling system that it says can help data centres reduce water consumption while improving efficiency.
Article Summary: Polaroid has launched a new advertising campaign highlighting concerns over the water consumption of data centres that power AI technologies. The company recently unveiled a billboard at Coney Island Beach in Brooklyn, New York, carrying the message: "The best of summer is analog"

Article Title: India’s factories are getting smarter; The companies that act now will own the next decade
Article Text:
Source: MarketsandMarkets, India AI in Manufacturing Market Report, 2025-2030

Predictive Maintenance: The End of Unplanned Downtime

Source: NASSCOM/MeitY Survey cited in India AI Landscape 2025 Index Report, AISpectrumIndia, 2025

Quality Control: Seeing What the Human Eye Cannot

Source: MarketResearchFuture, India AI in Manufacturing Market, 2025; Company announcements

Supply Chain Intelligence: From Reactive to Predictive

Source: MarketsandMarkets, India AI in Supply Chain Market, 2026

The Make in India Imperative

Source: IBM Press Release, September 2025; Government of India Make in India initiative

What AI Quotient Means for Indian Manufacturers

India’s manufacturing sector contributes 17 per cent of GDP and employs over 27 million people. A new wave of AI deployment is transforming how Indian factories predict failure, ensure quality, and optimise supply chains. The question for every manufacturer is not whether to adopt AI, but whether they are moving fast enough.For most of its post-independence history, Indian manufacturing has competed on the basis of labour cost, scale, and geographic advantage. These remain important but they are increasingly insufficient. Global competition in precision manufacturing, pharmaceuticals, automotive and electronics is increasingly being won not on the factory floor but in the data layer above it: in the quality of demand forecasting, the sophistication of predictive maintenance, the precision of quality control, and the resilience of supply chain management.This is the terrain on which AI is reshaping Indian manufacturing. The India AI in Manufacturing market was valued at $860 million in 2025 and is projected to reach $4.89 billion by 2030, representing a compound annual growth rate of 41.5 per cent – one of the fastest growth rates of any sector in the Indian economy.In traditional manufacturing environments, machines are serviced on a schedule: every 500 hours, every 30 days, or after a breakdown.Each of these approaches has the same fundamental flaw – they either over-maintain equipment, creating unnecessary cost, or under-maintain it, creating unplanned downtime that disrupts production schedules and erodes margins.AI-powered predictive maintenance systems change this calculus entirely. By continuously monitoring sensor data from machinery – vibration, temperature, acoustic signatures, power consumption – these systems can identify the early signs of component failure days or weeks before it occurs, allowing maintenance to be scheduled precisely when it is needed and not a moment before.According to a NASSCOM and MeitY survey, 65 per cent of large Indian manufacturers had adopted some form of AI by 2024, up from 45 per cent in 2022. Predictive maintenance is among the most widely deployed applications, with documented reductions in unplanned downtime of between 30 and 50 per cent in well-implemented deployments.Computer vision-based quality control is among the most immediately impactful AI applications in manufacturing. Traditional quality inspection relies on human visual inspection — a process that is accurate to approximately 80 per cent on a fresh inspector at the start of a shift, but degrades significantly over the course of a day as fatigue sets in. Defect rates that pass human inspection can reach 5 to 10 per cent in high-volume production environments.AI-powered vision systems inspect every product on the production line in real time, identifying defects with accuracy rates that consistently exceed 99 per cent. For sectors where quality non-compliance carries regulatory consequences — pharmaceuticals, food processing, automotive components — this is a risk management imperative.In October 2025, Siemens launched a unique AI-based quality control system for the Indian manufacturing sector to enhance product quality and minimise wastage. This followed a TCS partnership in December 2025 with a leading automotive manufacturer to deploy AI-driven predictive maintenance, and Wipro’s November 2025 launch of an AI platform for supply chain optimisation in manufacturing.The supply chain disruptions of the early 2020s revealed the fragility of manufacturing operations built on just-in-time inventory models and single-source supplier relationships. AI-driven supply chain management addresses this fragility by creating systems that can model and anticipate disruption before it occurs.The India AI in Supply Chain Market is projected to grow at a CAGR of 37.8 per cent through 2032. Manufacturers are deploying machine learning models that simultaneously incorporate demand signals, supplier performance data, logistics capacity, commodity price trends, and geopolitical risk indicators to produce supply chain recommendations that no human analyst could generate with equivalent speed or accuracy.For India’s pharmaceutical manufacturers, AI-driven supply chain intelligence is enabling compliance with increasingly stringent cold-chain requirements. For FMCG companies, it is reducing inventory carrying costs while improving service levels. For automotive manufacturers navigating the semiconductor supply complexity of an electric vehicle transition, it is becoming existential.The Government of India’s Make in India campaign targets manufacturing’s share of GDP at 25 per cent. Achieving this in a global environment where competitors in Vietnam, Mexico, and China are also aggressively deploying AI in manufacturing requires Indian manufacturers to close the AIQ gap, and to do so quickly. In September 2025, IBM announced a strategic partnership with the Indian government under the IndiaAI Mission, specifically focused on deploying AI solutions across Indian manufacturing sectors to enhance productivity and competitiveness.A high-AIQ manufacturing organisation is one that has moved from reactive to predictive across its core operations. Its maintenance is data-driven. Its quality control is AI-powered. Its supply chain is dynamically optimised. Its energy management is intelligent. And its leadership team treats operational data not as a reporting tool but as a strategic asset.The TOI AI Quotient Awards recognises Indian manufacturers who have made this transition — who have raised the intelligence of their operations to a level that creates durable competitive advantage. The award is not for the largest deployment or the most expensive system. It is for the most impactful one.
Article Summary: India’s manufacturing sector contributes 17 per cent of GDP and employs over 27 million people. A new wave of AI deployment is transforming how Indian factories predict failure, ensure quality, and optimise supply chains. The India AI in Manufacturing market was valued at $860 million in 2025 and is projected to reach $4.89 billion by 2030.

Article Title: Gruner Renewable Energy's Satna plant claims setting Asia record with 23.5 TPD CBG output
Article Text:
Representative image

Gruner Renewable Energy, the flagship company of the Gruner Group, claimed to have achieved a milestone in India’s clean energy journey, recording 23.5 tonnes per day (TPD) of Compressed BioGas (CBG) production at its Satna facility. The company claims that this is the highest ever output by any CBG plant in Asia. "Gruner Renewable Energy has established a new benchmark by recording an unprecedented 23.5 tonnes per day (TPD) of CBG production in a single day at Satna—the highest ever achieved by any CBG facility in Asia. The BioGas plant built and operated by Gruner is operating at 120% of its designed production capacity, significantly outperforming industry utilization levels, where many CBG facilities continue to operate at only 40–50% of installed capacity," said the company in a release.Commenting on the achievement, Utkarsh Gupta, founder & CEO, Gruner Renewable Energy, said, "Achieving 23.5 TPD of CBG production and operating at 120% of our designed capacity is a defining milestone not only for Gruner Renewable Energy but for India's entire bioenergy sector. This achievement demonstrates that India has the capability to build and operate world-class renewable energy infrastructure while transforming agricultural waste into clean energy, farmer prosperity, and greater energy security for the nation.Mehmet Oenal, COO of Gruner New Inergie Deutschland GMBH, added, "This achievement is far greater than a corporate milestone—it represents a defining moment for India's bioenergy sector. The record-setting performance of our Satna CBG Plant demonstrates that India possesses the capability, expertise, and vision to build and operate renewable energy infrastructure that can compete with the very best in the world. "
Article Summary: Gruner Renewable Energy recorded 23.5 tonnes per day (TPD) of Compressed BioGas (CBG) production at its Satna facility. The company claims that this is the highest ever output by any CBG plant in Asia. The BioGas plant is operating at 120% of its designed production capacity.

Article Title: Wikipedia blocks founder Larry Sanger, who praised Elon Musk's Grokepedia, from the platform after he called for more balanced ...
Article Text:
Representative Image

Sanger says ‘faceless mob’ controlling Wikipedia

Wikipedia temporarily blocked its co-founder, Larry Sanger, from editing pages on the platform, a report has claimed, adding that the block was handed down following Sanger’s demand of more balance, fairness and intellectual diversity on the heavily criticised website. Sanger co-founded the online encyclopedia alongside Jimmy Wales in 2001, coined the name “Wikipedia,” and drafted its original foundational rules.According to a report by The New York post, he expressed shock over the decision, saying, “I am flabbergasted.” The feud reportedly ignited last month when Sanger launched WikiProject Intellectual Diversity (WID) initiative that has been designed to reinforce Wikipedia’s “original, firm commitment to intellectual diversity” by focusing on neutrality and transparency.While the exact official reason for the block was not provided, Sanger described the modern Wikipedia editing community as acting like a “mob or a blob” where users are loyal to each other rather than a shared vision of objective rules.“Even a lot of the hard and fast policies are regarded as just guidelines if everybody is on board,” Sanger was quoted as saying.While Wikipedia claims its content is democratically managed by hundreds of thousands of volunteers – citing 267,000 active contributors over the past month, Sanger argues that the control sits with a small, secretive group of administrators he calls the “Power 62.”He claims that 85% of these powerful accounts hide entirely behind anonymous screennames.The campaign to oust Sanger was reportedly instigated by an anonymous, highly controversial editor known on the platform as “TarnishedPath.” The ban on Wikipedia’s co-founder caused a stir within the community with several internal editors quickly interjecting to question the appropriateness of the block. Following the immediate public backlash after Sanger exposed the block on X (formerly Twitter) on Monday, his editing account was quietly reinstated.
Article Summary: Sanger says ‘faceless mob’ controlling Wikipedia editing community. Wikipedia temporarily blocked its co-founder, Larry Sanger, from editing pages on the platform. Sanger co-founded the online encyclopedia alongside Jimmy Wales in 2001, coined the name “Wikipedia,” and drafted its original foundational rules.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Steve Jobs: "Sometimes life hits you in the head with a…"
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Steve Jobs

Quote of the day by Steve Jobs

"Sometimes life hits you in the head with a brick. Don't lose faith."



Why Steve Jobs' most famous speech was about failure, not success

The story behind the quote by Steve Jobs

What is the meaning of the quote by Steve Jobs

Why this quote by Steve Jobs is relevant

How to apply this quote in daily life



Let yourself feel the blow, then keep moving. There is no need to pretend a setback does not hurt. Acknowledge it honestly, then take the next small step anyway.

Hold off on writing the ending. In the middle of a crisis it is tempting to decide your whole future is ruined. Remind yourself that you cannot yet see how things will turn out.

Look for what a setback might free up. Jobs found that losing his job opened space for new ideas. When something falls apart, ask quietly what it might make room for.

Lean on what you love. Jobs said the one thing that kept him going was loving his work. In hard times, returning to the people, work or passions you care about can carry you through.

Other famous quotes by Steve Jobs

"The only way to do great work is to love what you do."

"Your time is limited, so don't waste it living someone else's life."

"If you haven't found it yet, keep looking. Don't settle."

"Stay hungry. Stay foolish."

Life rarely warns you before it knocks you down. One moment things are going along just fine, and the next something blindsides you, a job loss, a failure, a setback you never saw coming. Steve Jobs knew that feeling well. In one of the most famous speeches ever given, he summed it up in a single blunt image. Sometimes, he said, life hits you in the head with a brick. His advice for those moments was simple and quietly powerful. Don't lose faith. Coming from a man who had been fired from the very company he built, only to come back stronger, the words carry real weight. They are not the easy comfort of someone who has had a smooth ride. They are the hard won conclusion of someone who got hit by the brick, kept going anyway, and was grateful later that he had.Steve Jobs was the co-founder of Apple and one of the most influential figures in modern technology. He helped create products that changed how the world uses computers, music and phones, and he later led Pixar, the animation studio behind Toy Story. He was known for his drive, his eye for design and his belief in doing work that mattered.He died in 2011.He said this particular line in 2005, in a commencement speech to graduating students at Stanford University. That speech has since become one of the most watched and quoted of its kind, partly because of how openly Jobs spoke about his failures rather than just his successes.What gives the line its power is what Jobs had just been describing. In 1985, at the age of thirty, he was forced out of Apple, the company he had started in his parents' garage. He later described it as devastating, a very public failure that left him unsure what to do next.Yet looking back, he came to see it as one of the best things that ever happened to him. Being pushed out, he said, freed him to start again, lighter and more creative. In the years that followed he built new companies and eventually returned to Apple. The brick, in other words, was getting fired. Don't lose faith was the attitude that carried him through to what came next.The quote works in two simple halves. The first half is honest about pain. Life, Jobs is saying, will sometimes hit you hard and without warning, like a brick to the head. He does not pretend that everything happens for a tidy reason or that setbacks do not hurt.The second half is where the hope sits. Don't lose faith. He is not telling you to ignore the blow or pretend you are fine. He is saying that even when things look their worst, it is worth holding on to the belief that you can get through it, and that something good may yet grow out of it.Almost everyone, sooner or later, gets hit by their own version of the brick. A lost job, a failed plan, a rejection, an illness, a relationship that ends. In those moments it is easy to decide that everything is ruined and to give up.Jobs's words are a reminder that the story is not over just because one chapter ended badly. His own life is the proof. The setback that felt like the end turned out to be a doorway to something better. This is not the hollow promise that every bad thing is secretly good. It is the more useful idea that losing faith too early can stop you from ever reaching the better thing that might be waiting on the other side.You do not need to run a company to use this. It is about how you handle the hard knocks everyone faces.The Stanford speech and his many talks left behind plenty of memorable lines. Here are a few of his genuine ones:What makes this quote land is that Jobs was not speaking in theory. He had taken the hit, felt the despair, and come out the other side with more than he started with. That is why don't lose faith is more than a feel good phrase coming from him. It is a report from someone who lived it. The next time life catches you off guard with a brick of its own, his words are worth holding on to. The blow is real, but so is the chance that one day you will be glad you kept going.
Article Summary: Steve Jobs: "Sometimes life hits you in the head with a brick. Don't lose faith" Jobs was the co-founder of Apple and later led Pixar, the animation studio behind Toy Story. He said the one thing that kept him going was loving his work.

Article Title: BP, Marathon, 7-Eleven, Walmart sued for allegedly using AI to boost prices at pumps in the State that pays highest gas prices in America
Article Text:
"While families struggle to afford the commute to work, defendants have conspired to put an end to competition, joining an AI-powered trust to ensure that no matter where a driver turns, the price for gasoline is artificially high,"

What the lawsuit alleges about gas station operating companies in US



Several major US fuel retailers have been sued by California drivers. According to a report by the news agency Reuters, the lawsuit alleges that the gas station operators used artificial intelligence (AI) to coordinate gasoline prices and raise prices at the pump. The lawsuit, filed in federal court in Sacramento, California, has been brought against gas station operators, including BP, Marathon Petroleum, Circle K, 7-Eleven, Walmart and Albertsons.The proposed class-action lawsuit claims the companies violated the state's Cartwright Act by using an AI-powered pricing tool developed by Kalibrate. According to the complaint, the software uses pricing data from competing gas stations to help coordinate fuel prices, reducing competition and leading to higher prices for consumers. The lawsuit also names Kalibrate as a defendant and alleges that the pricing practices violate California's Assembly Bill 325, a law that came into effect on January 1 and was aimed at addressing algorithmic price-fixing.the complaint said, as seen by Reuters.According to the complaint, fuel prices have increased by up to 30 cents per gallon in regions where a significant share of gas stations use Kalibrate's AI pricing tool. The lawsuit estimates that every one-cent increase in gasoline prices costs California drivers an additional $134 million annually.The plaintiffs claim that the alleged pricing coordination has contributed to higher fuel costs in California, which currently has the highest average gasoline prices in the United States. According to AAA data cited in the lawsuit, regular gasoline in California averages $5.58 per gallon, compared to the national average of $3.93 per gallon.The defendants collectively operate more than 1,700 gas stations across California, according to the complaint. The lawsuit seeks unspecified damages for drivers who allegedly paid higher gasoline prices due to the use of the AI-based pricing system. The companies named in the lawsuit either declined to comment or did not immediately respond to requests for comment, Reuters reported.
Article Summary: Lawsuit filed in federal court in Sacramento, California, has been brought against gas station operators. BP, Marathon Petroleum, Circle K, 7-Eleven, Walmart and Albertsons named in lawsuit. Lawsuit claims companies used artificial intelligence (AI) to coordinate gasoline prices.

Article Title: Infosys chairman Nandan Nilekani to company's shareholders: AI will not replace companies like ours, it will ...
Article Text:
Infosys CEO says deal pipeline strong

Indian Tech’s Nifty share shrinks to record low



Infosys Chairman Nandan Nilekani sent a 'calming message' to shareholders at the company's 45th Annual General Meeting (AGM). Addressing shareholders, Nilekani answered what has been the biggest worry of most analysts and shareholders about software companies. "AI will not replace companies like ours. It will amplify those who move with purpose and adapt with speed," Nilekani said. Infosys chairman asserted that the Bengaluru-headquartered company "is more relevant than ever" and eagerly eyeing around $300-400 billion in AI opportunities by 2030.Nilekani explained that enterprise solutions demand rigorous testing, resilient architecture, foundational cybersecurity, and strict data governance that cannot be outsourced to external platforms. He added that the current AI revolution has made legacy modernisation urgent, positioning Infosys to capitalise on this structural shift in technology."The defining opportunity lies in integrating intelligent AI systems with mission-critical enterprise platforms. The greatest value will come from combining the world of models and agents with traditional transaction systems that continue to underpin enterprise operations. That convergence is where the next wave of opportunities will emerge," Nilekani told shareholders in his address."Our clients trust Infosys to bring hard-earned learning to help them navigate the complexities of enterprise AI.Infosys is fully prepared to deliver on that trust and help our clients navigate the next," he said, adding that Infosys is already collaborating with 90 per cent of our top 200 clients in their AI journeys.Addressing shareholders, Infosys Chief Executive Officer and Managing Director Salil Parekh said that the company is aggressively expanding its footprint in AI, with its AI services revenue share growing at a fast pace. He noted that customers are increasingly looking to leverage AI across six broad areas: AI engineering and strategy, data, process efficiency, technology modernisation, physical AI (in manufacturing, medical, and automotive sectors), and AI trust and security.AI worries have led to India’s software exporters steadily losing their sway on the country’s stock market. Concerns over artificial intelligence-led disruption have triggered a prolonged selloff in the sector. The combined weight of five information technology companies in the NSE Nifty 50 Index has fallen below 7.6%, the lowest at least since 2002, according to data compiled by Bloomberg. At their peak more than two decades ago, the cohort accounted for more than a fifth of the benchmark. Technology is now the fifth-largest sector in the Nifty 50, trailing financials, consumer discretionary, energy and industrials. Infosys has slipped to be the eighth-largest stock in the Nifty 50 by weight from third place five years ago, whereas Tata Consultancy Services ranks 13th.
Article Summary: Infosys Chairman Nandan Nilekani sent a 'calming message' to shareholders at the company's 45th Annual General Meeting (AGM) "AI will not replace companies like ours. It will amplify those who move with purpose and adapt with speed," he said. Infosys is eagerly eyeing around $300-400 billion in AI opportunities by 2030.

Article Title: Beyond the Fable ban: Why tech diplomacy needs a ‘2+2+T’ upgrade
Article Text:
By

Abhivardhan, President of the Indian Society of Artificial Intelligence and Law and Founder, Indic Pacific

AND

Aditya Jakki, Policy & Media Lead at Bharat Pacific, Indian Society of Artificial Intelligence and Law.

It clearly seems that the contemporary global order has decisively moved beyond unipolar or even conventional multipolar structures, transitioning into an immensely complex heteropolar reality. Just three days after the launch of Fable 5 by Anthropic, US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick issued an unprecedented export control directive to Anthropic citing national security authorities. Because there is no reliable way to instantly verify the citizenship of hundreds of millions of users in real-time, Anthropic was forced to shut off access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 entirely for everyone, including American citizens, to ensure compliance. This shows two major trajectories in technology policy. The first trajectory means that governments might act to become more reactive. While some view this as standard digital protectionism, it matters for a much deeper reason: it shatters the illusion of “weightless digitalization.” The second trajectory means that the element of trust among nations to cooperate on B2C and B2B digital supply and value chains will not remain the same.This was even reflected in the Telegram Ban case in the Delhi High Court; in such instances, the mere removal of illicit material fails to address the foundational question of whether a platform's architectural design inherently facilitates the rapid, near-instantaneous reconstitution of the very same fraudulent networks.Within this shifting paradigm, geopolitical and geoeconomic power is heavily complimentarily dispersed among sovereign governments, quasi-sovereign tech giants, and highly decentralized digital networks. To navigate this era successfully, ensuring equitable AI diffusion requires India to fundamentally decouple technology diplomacy from the traditional parameters of economic statecraft. Let’s understand this through the example of a digital cloud economy.The Physical Fragility of the “Cloud”The concept of the "cloud" implies an ethereal, borderless expanse, but the reality is that the digital economy is anchored to the ocean floor. Over 95% of international data transmission, the very lifeblood of global AI infrastructure, has historically traveled through multi-terabit undersea fiber-optic cables. These critical conduits connect Europe, Africa, and the Indo-Pacific, creating an intricate web of digital dependency. In fact, the crisis around the Strait of Hormuz directly mirrors the systemic vulnerabilities of these digital cloud ecosystems. If a cable snaps in the Persian Gulf or the Red Sea, sending a slow-moving, highly specialized vessel into a free-fire zone requires complex military escorts. Furthermore, when cables in a chokepoint get damaged, the sheer volume of data in the Middle East corridor rapidly exceeds the capacity of alternative routes. Although the internet is designed to reroute traffic, this bottleneck effect inevitably leads to extreme network congestion, packet loss, and latency.Traditional Economic Statecraft and the Importance of DeepTech EcosystemIt is important to understand that conventional mechanisms of statecraft around technology per se, and how they are fundamentally inadequately equipped.For instance, traditional trade bureaus and commerce ministries are structurally designed to chase immediate export and import victories. This foundational design makes them entirely the wrong vehicles for negotiating the architecture of algorithmic governance. Even otherwise, conventional foreign policy circles and commentators propose managing frontier AI risks like the Fable 5 ban by routing chatbot diplomacy through traditional multilateral bodies like the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) or by negotiating bilateral “trusted corridors”. In theory, this makes perfect sense. Leaning on established diplomatic channels and consensus-driven forums feels like the mature, tested way to handle global tech governance. The problem, however, is that the ITU is a slow-moving UN standards body. It operates on a slow, one-nation-one-vote UN bureaucracy. Export controls, by contrast, are rapid-deployment denial strategies designed to starve adversaries of strategic assets. Further, the ITU is currently the exact battleground where China and Russia are actively trying to rewrite global internet protocols to legitimise their approach towards state control over data. Funnelling frontier AI diplomacy through this body hands leverage directly to the nations these export controls are designed to constrain. Furthermore, the US directive targeted all foreign nationals, proving that multilateral negotiations and “trusted partner” statuses may not offer reliable benefits.Now, in the economic front of technology, for decades, the dominant corporate model has been optimized for low-capital-expenditure, high-margin service delivery. Why should a legacy enterprise risk billions of dollars on highly speculative, ten-year scientific research arcs when it can operate on safe, predictable, quarterly return-on-investment (ROI) cycles? Even if the state were to extend ultra-cheap credit, offer subsidized land and electricity, and provide aggressive corporate tax rebates for R&D, there is deep skepticism that legacy industry players would choose to operate at such low ROI or sacrifice quarterly dividends to build world-beating, foundational infrastructure. Consequently, traditional industry chambers and legacy corporate forums no longer represent the structural survival interests of the emerging deep-tech and AI startup ecosystem.This is precisely where economic diplomacy requires a fundamental upgrade. In this century, deeptech and AI startups serve as the primary instruments driving a state’s industrial policy. National champions certainly hold value; however, securing supply chain and value chain sovereignty in the AI space remains economically unachievable without these agile innovation engines—especially as a developing nation like India focuses on stratifying its strategic avenues.The ‘2+2+T’ Diplomatic ArchitectureResident powers like India must evolve from digital services economies into infrastructure states. This pivot is already underway domestically. According to the 360 ONE Panorama 2026 Report, the physical foundation for this shift is visible in the labor market: manufacturing employment has officially overtaken construction as a non-agricultural employer. While agriculture retains 266 million workers, the non-agricultural segment has swelled to 352 million.However, as the state executes this shift, it cannot replicate old dependencies in the digital realm. Industrial policies must avoid over-subsidizing hardware supply chains that remain tightly coupled to foreign Large Language Models. Instead, state funding must prioritize deep-tech startups building branched compute alternatives and decentralized AI architectures that bypass legacy WENA silicon.Yet, even a robust domestic manufacturing base offers no protection if international trade frameworks force local digital markets open on predatory terms. Because AI supply chains operate in a gray zone between military defense and commercial trade, they require a diplomatic upgrade.This necessitates a "2+2+T" ministerial model.While conventional defense and foreign ministries—the "2+2" foundation—secure physical territory and geopolitical alliances, governments must establish a permanent "T" (Technology) pillar. This means empowering technical plenipotentiaries to secure digital borders. Operating in an insulated Track 1 lane that bypasses slow multilateral bureaucracies, this pillar’s mandate is state-to-state AI security and deep-tech coordination. It ensures algorithmic sovereignty is never traded away for short-term commodity access.
Article Summary: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick issued an unprecedented export control directive to Anthropic citing national security authorities. Anthropic was forced to shut off access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 entirely for everyone, including American citizens, to ensure compliance. This shows two major trajectories in technology policy.

Article Title: Meta Glasses with EssilorLuxottica lenses launched in the US
Article Text:
Meta Glasses features

Facebook-parent Meta has launched new Meta Glasses in association with EssilorLuxottica. The company claims that this new line of smart glasses feature bold and timeless shapes, rich colors, and premium materials, all consciously crafted and starting at just $299. Meta has also introduced a collection designed in collaboration with Kylie Jenner. The company has launched three type of designs for the which include:* Meta Adventurer — A clean rectangle shape for a timeless, versatile look. Available in Standard and Large.* Meta Fury — A bold frame that makes a bold statement.* Meta Glasses by Kylie — A unique slim oval frame inspired by Kylie’s personal style.Meta Glasses also come with a palette of colours, including Classic Black, Classic Tortoise, Racing Green, Linen, Merlot, Mahogany, and Sandstone, with sun, Transitions®, polarized, and clear lens options developed by EssilorLuxottica.Meta Glasses are also compatible with prescription lenses*, and with the new Rx Lens Swap, customers can add prescription lenses with their preferred optician after the point of purchase, allowing customers to add prescription without voiding the warranty.From building in 3-way adjustable nose pads, adjustable temple tips, and overextension hinges to the latest Meta AI technology, we considered every detail to deliver AI glasses you'll love, with more ways to personalise your fit than ever before. And like all AI glasses in our portfolio, Meta Glasses will feature:* Meta AI at your fingertips — A dedicated action button lets you quickly invoke Meta AI or customise it to launch your favourite feature.* Crystal-clear audio — Open-ear speakers deliver pristine audio quality without blocking your ears, so you can enjoy phone calls, music, podcasts and audiobooks while staying present and aware of your surroundings.* Advanced multi-mic array — Advanced wind noise reduction means calls, messaging, and voice control are always crisp and sharp.* Capture and share — Take photos and videos hands-free to stay present in the moment.* Privacy first — Meta Glasses have clear, easy settings that help you control what you choose to share with others, as well as built-in safeguards to respect the privacy of people around you.* Power through your day — Over 8 hours of battery so you can go from morning to night.Meta Glasses are available starting today at Meta. com, Lenscrafters, Sunglasses Hut, Best Buy, Amazon, and additional select retailers.
