Meta has also introduced a collection designed in collaboration with Kylie Jenner.

Article Title: After 9 years, Chinese supercomputer beats America's in world's fastest supercomputer ranking; telling US: We really don't need your GPUs
Article Text:
How a CPU-only machine leapfrogged El Capitan and the US national labs



Why Beijing wanted the world to see this win



The catch: this crown isn't the AI crown



China just pulled off something it hasn't managed since 2017. A supercomputer called LineShine, housed at the National Supercomputing Center in Shenzhen, has been crowned the world's fastest machine on the latest TOP500 list. And it did so without a single chip from Nvidia, AMD, or Intel. The system clocked 2.198 exaflops—more than 2 quintillion calculations every second—and knocked America's El Capitan off the throne it had held since November 2024. The kicker? LineShine runs entirely on CPUs, the ordinary processors most supercomputers ditched years ago in favor of GPUs. For Washington, which has spent years choking off China's access to advanced AI chips, that's an uncomfortable message.LineShine's numbers came in more than 20% faster than El Capitan, which sits at the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory and helps maintain the US nuclear weapons stockpile. El Capitan now ranks second, followed by two more American machines in Tennessee and Illinois. Germany's Jupiter slid to fifth. Those five remain the only publicly verified exascale computers on Earth.The design itself is what caught attention. LineShine packs nearly 14 million computing cores across 90 cabinets, drawing about 42.2 megawatts of power.Its LX2 processors are a homegrown effort built on Armv9 designs—licensed from Britain's Arm Holdings—and run KylinOS, a Chinese Linux distribution. Each chip bundles in specialized circuitry for matrix and vector math, the kind of work GPUs usually handle. Jack Dongarra, a TOP500 organizer who inspected the machine, called it impressive and noted it isn't reliant on GPUs at all.Here's the interesting part: China stopped submitting machines to the TOP500 back in 2023, after years of US export controls. So putting LineShine forward was a choice. "I'm not surprised it's the number one system. What I'm surprised by is that they submitted it," said Addison Snell of Intersect360 Research. Dongarra was reportedly told the machine was built without government funding, which is why its designers felt free to enter it. They've also submitted 14 entries for the Gordon Bell Prize, science's big computing award.The timing isn't subtle. It lands as President Trump pushes his own tech agenda, having just signed an executive order targeting a US lead in quantum computing.Before anyone declares China the AI superpower, the experts have caveats. LineShine ranked only fourth on a benchmark built to mimic AI-style computing. And the biggest American AI systems—xAI's Colossus, plus clusters from Microsoft, Amazon, and Google—simply don't bother entering the TOP500. One study suggested Colossus already outguns El Capitan. "If the hyperscalers submitted their systems, this 'world's fastest' would not crack the top five," said Jimmy Goodrich of UC's Institute on Global Conflict and Cooperation. He also flagged CPUs as a loophole in current export rules—one Washington may move to close.
Article Summary: China's LineShine supercomputer is the fastest on the TOP500 list. It clocked 2.198 exaflops, more than 2 quintillion calculations every second. The machine runs entirely on CPUs, the ordinary processors most supercomputers ditched years ago in favor of GPUs.

Article Title: In 1992, Eric Fossum tried to reduce spacecraft image noise and created the CMOS sensor behind smartphone cameras
Article Text:
Eric R. Fossum (born October 17, 1957) is an American engineer. Currently, he is a professor at Thayer School of Engineering in Dartmouth College. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

The trick that decreased the noise

An active-pixel sensor (APS) is an image sensor where each pixel sensor unit cell has a photodetector and one or more active transistors. Image Credit: Wikipedia

From space vehicles to mobile phones

An enduring effect on everyday life

Each time you use a mobile phone camera, you are using technology originally developed for space applications. In 1990, Eric Fossum joined NASA ’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory. He later developed a technology that went beyond space exploration and eventually revolutionized consumer photography. He focused on a practical challenge at NASA: finding ways to make spacecraft imaging systems smaller and more energy-efficient for interplanetary space missions.Although these devices provided images of superior quality, they were rather large-sized, costly, and energy-intensive. Therefore, NASA needed another type of technology for its missions in space. It was this need that prompted NASA to focus on CMOS technology.But early CMOS sensors had an enormous problem – they generated excessive amounts of visual noise. This noise was distracting and could make images difficult to use for scientific study. According to the historical review posted on NASA's science website , Fossum was hired at JPL in 1990 to advance CCD technology for interplanetary missions. Fossum focused on engineering requirements rather than fashion or price, helping to open a new direction for electronic sensors.In order to reduce the amount of noise, Fossum came up with the idea of a CMOS active pixel sensor. Conventional sensors could distort the signal as the image was read out from the sensor to the computer. Fossum solved this problem by using the technique of intra-pixel charge transfer together with correlated double sampling.In simple terms, the technique sampled each pixel twice to reduce background noise before the data were read out. The innovation was an enormous success. It enabled engineers to fit all the required camera parts onto a single small silicon chip. NASA engineers began to refer to this innovation as a camera on a chip. It meant cameras could operate without external circuits.Technical papers published by NASA in the middle of the 1990s revealed that miniature CMOS sensors were quickly developing into real hardware from purely experimental lab work. The new chips started competing with the older and energy-consuming CCDs. In fact, this change significantly influenced the design of digital imaging equipment. These new sensors were highly compact and consumed minimal amounts of battery power. They were thus suitable for cramped locations. Although it was first used for space vehicles, this feature turned out to be exactly what consumer electronics manufacturers needed.According to the official NASA Spinoff report , this active-pixel design was eventually made into NASA's most pervasive spin-off technology. It would go on to become a dominant technology in the digital imaging industry. As cameras became cheaper and smaller, they were integrated into portable mobile devices.The technology has become so commonplace today that its complex underlying chip is used without people realizing it at all, even in applications other than mobile phones. These include action cameras, webcams, high-definition cameras, and medical devices such as digital dental X-ray systems.This technical project to reduce noise in astronomical imaging helped pave the way for modern visual communication technologies through Eric Fossum's work at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory.
Article Summary: Eric Fossum is a professor at Thayer School of Engineering in Dartmouth College. In 1990, he joined NASA ’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory. He later developed a technology that went beyond space exploration and eventually revolutionized consumer photography. Fossum focused on engineering requirements rather than fashion or price, helping to open a new direction for electronic sensors.

Article Title: In 1970, Robert Metcalfe, sketching a bus for office computers, unexpectedly created Ethernet
Article Text:
Robert Metcalfe National Medal of Technology. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

A young researcher

Solving the office wire mess

The original Xerox PARC ethernet cable, part of an exhibit at the Computer History Museum in Mountain View, California. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

From a prototype to a worldwide standard

The enduring force of simplicity

Any time you connect your laptop to a router or watch a video while you work, you are using the network system that powers the modern world. This network system is called Ethernet. Although Ethernet is now an integral part of modern life, its origins were anything but high-tech or corporate. To the contrary, the birth of such important invention began with a humble sketch on a piece of paper. When computers were still new, engineer Robert Metcalfe sat down to solve a problem and sketch an important idea.To see the roots of his success, trace the story back to 1970. Back then, there was no such thing as the internet, computers were huge devices which communicated with each other very rarely. Robert Metcalfe was a young researcher at the start of his academic career. In accordance with the Lemelson-MIT biographical profile of Robert Metcalfe , in 1970 he studied at Harvard University and pursued his master's degree. That was an important time in his life; however, he had not yet become known for developing Ethernet. His studies on packet-switching and interconnection continued over the next few years.During this period, Metcalfe became fascinated by how different machines communicate. A bus is like a single main street in a city, where every computer can access the same line. It was an important engineering advance because engineers initially assumed that every computer needed a direct point-to-point line.The early bus drawing of Metcalfe demonstrated much more efficient way of dealing with data.The basic problem of local networking was easy to describe but difficult to solve. If there were ten computers in an office, connecting them individually required many different cables. As a result, offices and laboratories quickly became tangled in cables that could not easily be expanded. Metcalfe realized that the problem could be solved by using a shared-medium approach. So, instead of numerous point-to-point lines it would be enough to connect machines and office devices to one line.This approach meant that all computers would transmit information through one shared line. In practical terms, it was compact and easy to expand as new devices were added. When an enterprise decided to buy a new computer, it did not have to install new lines to connect it with other computers. All it needed to do was to hook the computer to the central common line. The bus structure was well suited to offices and laboratories, where computers often shared the same floor or building.In the 1970s, these ideas were still theoretical and largely confined to universities. Later, Metcalfe joined Xerox PARC, where his idea became a prototype in 1973. At that point, the system was named Ethernet. Nevertheless, a laboratory prototype needs to be introduced to the scientific community before it can have a wider impact.A few years later, the system was thoroughly documented so that other engineers could review it. As it was noted in the 1976 research paper by Robert Metcalfe and David Boggs , Ethernet was officially announced as a branching broadcast communication system for local computer networks. This document shows how the sketch evolved into a working system architecture. The paper explains how multiple users can send data through the same channel without colliding.It is remarkable how enduring the original design idea has been. Most technologies become outdated within a few years, but the principle behind Metcalfe's first sketch remains relevant today. In the 1980s, commercial Ethernet systems began to spread, and they became a worldwide standard because of their simplicity and openness to adoption by other manufacturers.Metcalfe's early work shows that technological revolutions do not happen overnight or arrive fully formed. Instead, such a revolution often begins with small steps by a researcher trying to simplify a difficult problem on paper. Metcalfe's first step - a rough sketch of a bus in 1970 - helped lay the groundwork for Ethernet.
Article Summary: Robert Metcalfe was a young researcher at the start of his academic career. In 1970 he studied at Harvard University and pursued his master's degree. His studies on packet-switching and interconnection continued over the next few years. During this period he became fascinated by how different machines communicate. His first step - a rough sketch of a bus - helped lay the groundwork for Ethernet.

Article Title: Two months after telling US staff "there is no option to opt out," Meta pauses its employee-tracking program; confirms the fear workers had from day one
Article Text:
Meta's MCI tracking tool leaked employee data companywide—now it's paused. (AP Photo/Noah Berger, File)

Meta's MCI spilled data internally, revealing employee activity to all

Why 1,600 Meta employees fought the AI tracking tool

Meta has hit pause on the program that quietly recorded its US employees' keystrokes, mouse clicks and screen content to train its AI—but not before the whole thing leaked. An internal security notice, first reported by WIRED, warned this week that data scooped up by the program had been left sitting in the open, accessible to anyone inside the company.The timing stings: it's been barely two months since Meta told staff there was no way to switch the tracking off.The tool goes by Model Capability Initiative, or MCI, and it landed on US employees' work laptops in April. It logged their inputs and grabbed periodic screenshots, all to teach Meta's AI agents how people actually navigate software—the dropdown menus, the keyboard shortcuts, the small stuff models still fumble.Opting out wasn't an option at launch. After weeks of pushback, Meta softened slightly, letting some staff pause the tracking for 30 minutes at a stretch.The notice went out Monday, after a Meta engineer flagged that MCI data had spilled internally. Per WIRED, the exposure spanned roughly 45,000 hive tables holding employee activity—full prompts and transcriptions, private conversations, even people and performance records.Business Insider, which got screenshots of the incident, reported Meta logged it as a SEV 2, the second rung on its five-point severity scale.Inside the company, people were not amused. "I am incensed," one employee wrote in an internal group, per Business Insider, angry the data hadn't been locked down the way Meta promised. Over in a channel where staff swap jokes, someone dropped a meme: "0 days since our last nonsense."A former employee who'd fought the program called the lapse "a mess," and said it was exactly what workers had been flagging for weeks.MCI drew fire well before it leaked. More than 1,600 employees signed a petition warning the data collection invited security and regulatory trouble, breaches included. Zuckerberg brushed past those concerns, telling staff in leaked audio that AI learns best by "watching really smart people do things"—and that Meta's own workforce beat any contractor it could hire.Meta insists nothing was improperly accessed. "We have carefully designed this program with privacy safeguards," spokesperson Tracy Clayton said, "and while we have no indication at this time that any data was improperly accessed by Meta employees, we're pausing it while we investigate."CTO Andrew Bosworth conceded the rollout missed the bar its own privacy review had set.It caps a brutal stretch for Meta—mass layoffs, an AI reorg Bosworth himself called "atrocious," and a roughly $140 billion AI bill. And it follows a rare note of contrition from the top: in a leaked memo this month, reported by Reuters, Zuckerberg told staff the company had "made mistakes and will almost certainly make more" as it pushes through its AI overhaul.
Article Summary: Meta's AI tracking tool leaked employee data companywide. The tool recorded keystrokes, mouse clicks and screen content to train its AI. More than 1,600 employees signed a petition warning the data collection invited security and regulatory trouble. Meta insists nothing was improperly accessed.

Article Title: In 1973, Charles Geschke and John Warnock built page software that eventually led to the PDF files we use today
Article Text:
The PDF file format icon. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Science and Health with Key to The Scriptures, 1910

Preserving the integrity of the layout

John Warnock (Adobe) and Charles Geschke in 1982 at the foundation of Adobe Systems. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Perfect for seamless sharing

As digital publishing was still in its infancy, one particular nuisance continued to plague computer owners. A computer must produce output that prints the same page on any machine. This problem led computer scientists Charles Geschke and John Warnock to a breakthrough. Starting in 1973, they developed software that described pages well enough to preserve layout and fonts across different computers. Today, the format may seem commonplace, but at the time it was a major shift in digital publishing.The problem was simple to describe but difficult to solve. On paper, a page layout was fixed once created. But in early computing, a document could print differently depending on the device, fonts, or printer used. Page description technology lets designers specify exactly how a page should look instead of supplying only the text. Because software focused on the page itself, it became possible to create document files that worked across very different operating systems.The technology developed gradually, not overnight. As per the Library of Congress , "PDF was an extension of a prior imaging model called PostScript that incorporated additional structure and navigation into the model."It is important to note that PostScript was developed earlier as "a programming language for describing the contents of pages independent of device characteristics." That earlier development met the needs of the time. Different printers, software systems, and document formats all had to work together at the time. Using an established technology avoided reinventing the wheel.The new format worked because it treated appearance and content as equally important. According to a peer-reviewed article available through PubMed , PDF was invented so that the formatted documents could be transferred from one computer to another, even if the receiving computer did not have the fonts and files needed for reading. In the early days of digital publishing, when layout depended on the receiving computer, the risk of layout distortion was high. This format solved the problem by preserving a fixed version of the document. It treated the layout as part of the message sent to the recipient.As a result, documents could be sent between computers without losing their layout. This feature transformed legal documents, official reports, forms, and academic journals by letting authors create a definitive version of how their work should appear to readers. It shifted document design from flexible screens to precise visual control.The format became popular worldwide for more than just technical reasons. It addressed a major problem in computing at the time. The problem was how to share a document and be sure the recipient saw it as intended. It is like locking the document’s layout so the spacing and images stay in place. It remains the final step in design work before a document leaves the author’s computer.
Article Summary: In 1973, computer scientists Charles Geschke and John Warnock developed software that described pages well enough to preserve layout and fonts across different computers. PDF was invented so that the formatted documents could be transferred from one computer to another, even if the receiving computer did not have the fonts and files needed for reading.

Article Title: US official admits that Anthropic broke into some of government's most sensitive and secure computer systems; says: In minutes ...
Article Text:
Tension with Trump administration



A US official has confirmed that Anthropic’s Mythos AI model uncovered vulnerabilities in some of the government’s most sensitive computer systems during a testing exercise, as reported by news agency AP. The report adds that the results of the testing exercise has raised fresh concerns about the power and risks of advanced artificial intelligence. The official speaking anonymously to the Associated Press, said the testing was conducted under Project Glasswing, an Anthropic-led initiative involving U.S. intelligence agencies and tech companies. Within hours, the Mythos model identified weaknesses in classified systems, though the official stressed this did not mean the AI exploited them.Democratic Sen. Mark Warner revealed during a June 11 Senate hearing that the tool “broke into almost all of our classified systems, not in weeks but in hours,” citing information from NSA and Cyber Command chief Gen. Joshua Rudd.The report adds that despite the cooperation on testing, relations between AI giant Anthropic and the Trump administration remain strained. Earlier this month, the Trump administration ordered Anthropic to block foreign nationals from accessing its latest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, citing cybersecurity fears.Anthropic complied by disabling the models for all customers, but said the directive was unnecessary. The move followed Trump’s executive order requiring federal vetting of advanced AI systems for national security risks before public release.The report further adds that more than 100 cybersecurity executives and experts including leaders from Adobe and Nvidia urged the administration to lift restrictions, warning that sidelining Anthropic’s models could weaken U.S. defenses. They argued Mythos is effective at finding flaws but “not uniquely good” compared to other foundation and open-source models.
Article Summary: Anthropic's Mythos AI model uncovered vulnerabilities in some of the government's most sensitive computer systems during a testing exercise. The testing was conducted under Project Glasswing, an Anthropic-led initiative involving U.S. intelligence agencies and tech companies. Within hours, the Mythos model identified weaknesses in classified systems, though the official stressed this did not mean the AI exploited them.

Article Title: In 1965, E. A. Johnson described the touchscreen that shaped the touch displays we use today
Article Text:
A touchscreen (or touch screen) is a type of display that can detect touch input from a user to do a specific task. Image Credit: Wikipedia

How to Cope with a Stressful Job

The mechanics behind the technology of 1965

A radar display is an electronic device that presents radar data to the operator. Image Credit: Wikipedia

Why did the world have to wait

A legacy in our hands

Each time you press buttons on your smartphone or write messages on a tablet or even order food via a digital kiosk, you deal with a technology that seems natural to use. It is tempting to think that such touchscreens were created by modern technology giants just for fun. Nevertheless, it turns out that the history of touchscreens goes way back to 1965, and not in some fashionable design agency in Silicon Valley but in a British radar room.Engineer E. A. Johnson was employed at the Royal Radar Establishment located in Malvern, UK. He tried to find a solution to the problem related to air traffic controllers' interaction with computers. Johnson’s insight was simple: if a display already shows the information operators need, why make them use a separate control?During the mid-sixties of the twentieth century, air traffic control was a tough and stressful profession. The operators were required to monitor the swiftly moving aircraft via the radar screens generated by the computer system. In order to handle the information, it was essential to constantly shift attention from one place – the screen – to other equipment – keyboards, joysticks, and light pens. Such an arrangement caused an unpleasant lag between target recognition and action, thus making the whole procedure unnecessarily complex.It became clear to Johnson that uniting the screen for viewing and controls into a single surface would greatly increase the safety and efficiency of the process. As stated by the article indexed on PubMed in relation to ergonomics, the touch-sensitive screens received significant attention in stressful conditions of the air traffic control center due to their ability to tightly connect human attention and machine reaction.Johnson designed and made the primitive ancestor of the glass that we touch and control now. He invented not just an idea, but a concrete technical solution. The screen of a computer was overlaid with a grid of very thin wires. When a finger touched the screen, the electrical field of the wires was interrupted by the finger.According to the historical thesis from the University of Glasgow , Johnson utilized these capacitance changes to determine the exact point of touching by a finger. The thesis says Johnson believed the design would improve air traffic control and could later be adapted for broader use in computer screens.Though the idea was amazing, it did not conquer the world immediately. Early touch interfaces faced serious difficulties connected with precision, reliability of the system, and habits of users in a difficult workplace environment.The concept stayed experimental for many years. It took decades for manufacturing technology, materials, and computer hardware to catch up with the original vision of Johnson. The basic concept continued to evolve as technology improved. The concept kept returning as technology progressed enough to make it practical.Historians often trace a line from Johnson’s prototype to later touchscreens. Although today's smartphones are equipped with complex multi-touch and gesture-based controls that were invented years later, they still operate using the very same idea of touch sensing through capacitors.Although Johnson did not design every single detail of the modern smartphone on his own, he introduced the idea of the screen as an interactive tool. His creation marked the point at which a computer display changed from a passive object to one people could interact with directly. The idea helped pave the way for one of the most common human-computer interfaces.
Article Summary: E. A. Johnson created the first touch screen in 1965. He wanted to improve the efficiency of air traffic controllers' interaction with computers. The concept stayed experimental for many years. It took decades for manufacturing technology, materials, and computer hardware to catch up with Johnson's vision.

Article Title: In 1996, Ajay Bhatt worked with Intel engineers to create USB ports still used in devices today
Article Text:
Ajay V. Bhatt is an Indian-American computer architect who produced several widely used technologies, including USB (Universal Serial Bus), Platform Power Management architecture, and various other chipsets. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Complexity of early computer technology

Intel's USB work in 1996

Serial and parallel ports connect devices efficiently safely. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Why one simple standard won the world

A permanent part of our lives

Every day, millions of people plug cables into their laptops, phones, or printers without thinking about it. However, in the early 1990s, connecting any auxiliary equipment to a personal computer was a nightmare. At that time, computers were filled with multiple incompatible ports and cords. The situation radically changed in 1996, when Ajay Bhatt, an engineer from Intel, joined forces with his colleagues to solve this daily challenge.Before USB, computer users had to deal with both parallel and serial ports. All devices had incompatible plugs, making it difficult for hardware engineers and regular users to set up even a simple computer. If a person wanted to connect a mouse, a keyboard, and a printer, he or she had to use three different cords. According to a peer-reviewed article indexed in PubMed , in January 1996, the first version of the Universal Serial Bus, or USB 1.0, appeared on the market.During that time, each new product would come along with its own set of strict rules and specific interface designs. Moreover, according to the study, the industry predicted the possibility of USB replacing a variety of serial and parallel ports, which clearly shows the magnitude of the changes occurring in the technological sphere. The tech industry decided to switch from a multitude of complex interfaces to one universal standard.The history of USB began when Ajay Bhatt and Intel engineers began designing a simpler connector. That fits the timeline, since the first version of USB appeared that same year. The idea was not to create a new device but to simplify connectivity. The goal was to ease the process of connecting devices.Over the next few decades, this practical solution evolved from an office drawing into an enormous global standard. A separate research paper published in 2025 on Sensors identifies USB as the de facto standard for connecting computers and their peripherals. USB became the standard gradually as it was adopted across many hardware platforms.USB became widely popular because it solved a common problem. The users required much less variety of ports to use, whereas hardware manufacturers had to have a much simpler and cheaper way of connecting their accessories. Being designed for handling routine computer operations rather than a particular accessory, it gained a huge advantage over its predecessors.Once consumers saw that one cable could handle multiple tasks, adoption accelerated. The change in perception has been permanent for consumers, and many users now expect devices to connect easily.An engineering idea that began with desktop computers soon made its way into every sphere of our lives. USB connectors soon found use beyond desktop computers and appeared in laptops, cameras, game consoles, and mobile phone chargers. This was possible because USB made device connections simple and unobtrusive.It is an example of how a simple idea helped shape modern digital connectivity.
Article Summary: In the early 1990s, connecting any auxiliary equipment to a personal computer was a nightmare. Ajay Bhatt, an engineer from Intel, joined forces with his colleagues to solve this daily challenge. The tech industry decided to switch from a multitude of complex interfaces to one universal standard.

Article Title: China hits back at US 'ban' on its tech companies with sanctions on 10 American firms; calls it response to US government’s "wrongful ..."
Article Text:
Representative Image

Finance ministry adds procurement ban

Response to US military company list

What is US Entity List

US-China technology rivalry continues

China has announced sanctions on 10 American military-linked companies on June 22, calling it a direct response to the US government’s “wrongful expansion” of restrictions on Chinese tech firms. According to a report by news agency AP, the Commerce Ministry said Chinese companies will be prohibited from exporting dual-use goods and items with both civilian and military applications to the sanctioned firms. The list includes drone makers, defense contractors and rare earth companies such as AVEOX, Red Cat Holdings, Teal Drones, IMSAR, Jaia Robotics, Ball Aerospace, Oshkosh Defense, L3Harris Maritime Services, MP Materials, and USA Rare Earth. The ministry added that this move was aimed at safeguarding China’s national security and countering US restrictions.Separately, China’s Finance Ministry barred government entities from purchasing products from 46 American companies, including units of Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, and General Dynamics. No reason was given, but analysts said the measure underscores Beijing’s intent to escalate pressure on U.S.defense-linked firms.Earlier this month, the U.S. Defense Department added several Chinese tech giants, including Alibaba and Baidu, to its list of firms allegedly tied to the Chinese military. The designation prevents them from securing U.S. defense contracts. Baidu called the claim “totally baseless.” China’s Commerce Ministry said the American sanctions run counter to the consensus reached between Xi Jinping and Donald Trump during Trump’s May visit to Beijing.George Chen of The Asia Group said the sanctions were “unsurprising and proportionate,” noting that most of the targeted U.S. firms already have limited business exposure in China. “The impact will be quite symbolic,” he added, but the move signals Beijing’s readiness to retaliate in the ongoing tech and defense standoff.According to Reuters, DeepSeek and over 100 other companies were approved last year for addition to the Entity List, a US trade blacklist that restricts access to American goods, software and technology. Companies placed on the list generally require special licenses to obtain US technology, and those licenses are often denied.Reuters reported that the Trump administration has held back publication of the new listings as it seeks to avoid escalating tensions with China.Sources told the news agency that at least 75 Chinese entities involved in advanced semiconductor production, chip equipment manufacturing and AI model development had been approved for blacklisting but are still awaiting publication. The delay comes as Washington and Beijing remain locked in a broader rivalry over technology, trade and national security.Reuters reported that Commerce Department officials have become cautious about adding Chinese firms to the blacklist due to concerns that new restrictions could further increase tensions between the two countries.
Article Summary: China has announced sanctions on 10 American military-linked companies on June 22. The list includes drone makers, defense contractors and rare earth companies. China’s Finance Ministry barred government entities from purchasing products from 46 American companies, including units of Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, and General Dynamics.

Article Title: Claude Code creator Boris Cherny who declared AI has solved coding 'admits' AI writing 100% code is getting 'problematic' as companies ...
Article Text:
AI writing all code creates new bottlenecks



Anthropic’s cost controls



Boris Cherny feels days of AI prompts are over



Boris Cherny, the creator of Anthropic’s generative coding tool Claude Code, has admitted that letting AI write 100% of code is becoming ‘problematic’ for companies, even as he continues to champion AI’s transformative role in software development. According to a report by Business Insider, speaking at a fireside chat hosted by Scale AI, Cherny said companies are right to focus on return on investment (ROI) as AI token costs rise.He responded directly to concerns raised by Uber COO Andrew Macdonald, who recently questioned whether AI spending was delivering enough consumer-facing value. “ROI is absolutely the right framing because you don’t want to just think about cost… you spend something on it and you get something back,” Cherny said. He cautioned against restricting token usage too early, arguing that experimentation often leads to breakthrough ideas.Cherny further acknowledged that measuring the impact of AI by the percentage of code written is no longer useful, since many engineers now allow AI to generate their code. “Once you get it to this point where engineers are just writing a lot of code, the bottleneck is going to be good ideas,” he said.He also warned that companies must focus on idea generation and innovation pipelines, rather than simply accelerating code output.Cherny also stressed on the fact that Anthropic offers enterprise customers ways to manage token budgets, including per-seat cost controls and backend usage monitoring. He also noted that tokens are not free for Anthropic either, “Every token we use is a token we do not give to a customer, so there’s an opportunity cost.”After previously declaring that “software engineering is dead,” Cherny now says the era of manually writing AI prompts is ending. According to a recent report by Business Insider, Cherny argues that the future lies in loop engineering, a system in which AI agents generate and refine prompts themselves. For those unaware, loops are the recurring systems that guide AI agents without constant human input. For instance, a command like /goal can instruct an AI model to keep working until a task is complete, rather than requiring step-by-step prompts. Cherny explained: “It’s an agent that prompts Claude. I don’t write the prompt anymore. Claude writes the prompt, and now I’m talking to that new Claude that is coordinating.”While loops reduce human effort, they raise concerns about token budgets. Running multiple agents and sub-agents can quickly become expensive. Steinberger advised using longer intervals — hourly or daily — to reduce costs, while Osmani cautioned that sub-agents should only be used when a second opinion is worth the expense.
Article Summary: Boris Cherny, the creator of Anthropic’s generative coding tool Claude Code, has admitted that letting AI write 100% of code is becoming ‘problematic’ for companies. “Once you get it to this point where engineers are just writing a lot of code, the bottleneck is going to be good ideas,” he said.

Article Title: After US restricts Anthropic use, CEO of Canadian tech startup backs ‘cutting ties’ with foreign AI providers: ‘This sentiment …’
Article Text:
Cohere CEO lists the risk of renting instead of owning



The CEO of Canadian artificial intelligence (AI) company Cohere is urging democratic nations to stop “renting” AI from foreign countries, warning that over-reliance on ‘external’ tech giants poses a massive national security threat. Aidan Gomez’s warning comes weeks after the US government restricted foreign access to Anthropic’s advanced “Mythos” AI model. Gomez says that the US restriction serves as a massive wake-up call for the rest of the world.“I think it’s woken everyone up to the reality, which is that centralized dependence on a single entity is a structural risk. You can absolutely just have access revoked…and services shut down,” Gomez stated.As AI increasingly becomes an integral part of modern critical infrastructure, Gomez argues that relying on foreign software interfaces (APIs) is an unacceptable vulnerability for independent nations. Gomez warns: “This sentiment of renting AI from someone rather than owning it is a national security risk. You need to fully control it.”The global AI landscape is currently dominated by the US and China. Outside of this duopoly, a tiny handful of prominent firms, such as Toronto-based Cohere and Paris-based Mistral, are capable of building and deploying large-scale frontier models independently.True “Sovereign AI” requires countries to control their systems end-to-end. According to Gomez, this means having domestic control over the entire supply chain of data centers, AI chips and the models.While fully independent AI is the ideal goal, it faces real-world limitations. Building and operating frontier-scale systems requires an extraordinary amount of capital, computing chips and electrical energy that most countries simply cannot afford on their own. He said that to achieve the goal, realistic sovereignty must be achieved through international alliances.“It’s not the case that every country is going to have each layer of the AI stack inside their own country…instead they need to find multiple partners for those layers. If we try to spread our resources, it just won’t work. We have to back champions and not spread our bets,” Gomez explained.
Article Summary: Aidan Gomez, CEO of Canadian artificial intelligence company Cohere, warns against'renting' AI from foreign countries. Gomez argues that relying on foreign software interfaces (APIs) is an unacceptable vulnerability for independent nations. True ‘Sovereign AI’ requires countries to control their systems end-to- end.
